        high := n - 1

            if abs_nums[mid] <= target {

                res = mid

                low = mid + 1

            } else {

                high = mid - 1

            }

        }

        count += int64(res - i)

    }

    return count

Now, the problem is to implement this in TypeScript. 

But note that the original nums can be up to 1e5 elements. So the code must be O(n log n) time. Sorting is O(n log n), and for each element, the binary search is O(log n), so overall O(n log n) time. Which is acceptable. 

Now, the code in TypeScript:

function solve(nums: number[]): number {
    const abs_nums = nums.map(x => Math.abs(x));
    abs_nums.sort((a, b) => a - b);
    let count = 0;
    for (let i = 0; i < abs_nums.length - 1; i++) {
        const target = 2 * abs_nums[i];
        let low = i + 1;
        let high = abs_nums.length - 1;
        let res = i; // initially, the index is i, but the subarray starts at i+1
        while (low <= high) {
            const mid = Math.floor((low + high) / 2);
            if (abs_nums[mid] <= target) {
                res = mid;
                low = mid + 1;
            } else {
                high = mid - 1;
            }
        }
        count += res - i;
    }
    return count;
}

Wait, but in the code, when the subarray is empty (i.e., when i is the last element), the loop is not entered. So for i = n-1, the loop is skipped. 

But the loop runs for i from 0 to n-2. Because in the for loop, i < abs_nums.length - 1. So for example, if the array has length 4, the loop runs for i=0,1,2 (since 3 is not less than 3).
